1:
"And Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes concerning the children of Israel, saying, This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded." The very brief verse notes Moses speaking the law of vows to the heads of the tribes. The grammatical voices note the introduction. Henry and Gill note that "Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes concerning the children of Israel, saying, This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded" — Moses delivered the law of vows to the leaders of the tribes, as God commanded; the Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes (the law given to the leaders, who would teach the people); the This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded (the divine authority of the law); the figure (the law of vows committed first to the heads of the tribes, that order be kept in its teaching; the truth that God's commands come with his authority, "this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded"; the figure of the orderly delivery of God's law through the appointed leaders; the law of vows, touching the binding of the word and the authority in the household, given by God).

On "Moses spake unto the heads of the tribes concerning the children of Israel," the witnesses note the leaders. Gill notes the law given to the heads of the tribes (the leaders taught) — the delivery (to the leaders).

On "saying, This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the authority: "This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded" — the divine authority of the law (God's command); the figure (the law given with God's authority); the providence (the law introduced, the binding of vows to follow, vv.2). The verse displays Moses speaking the law of vows to the heads of the tribes.

2:
"If a man vow a vow unto the LORD, or swear an oath to bind his soul with a bond; he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth." The witnesses note the binding of a man's vow and the sanctity of his word — the crux of the inviolable bond of the vow. On "he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth," interpreters expound the law. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the binding word — "if a man vow a vow unto the LORD, or swear an oath to bind his soul with a bond; he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth": a man who vows or swears to God is bound to perform it and must not break his word; the witnesses note the significance — the inviolable obligation of the vow (the man bound by his own word, the oath that binds the soul, the solemn duty to perform what is promised to God); the lesson — the sanctity of the word and the duty of keeping vows (the truth that what is freely vowed becomes a binding obligation, "when thou vowest a vow unto God, defer not to pay it... that which thou hast vowed... pay," Ecclesiastes 5:4-5; "better is it that thou shouldest not vow, than that thou shouldest vow and not pay"); the binding of the soul, the word once given to God not to be broken; the figure — the faithfulness that keeps its word, "he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not," Psalm 15:4; The figure of the inviolable bond of the word vowed to the LORD.

On "if a man vow a vow unto the LORD, or swear an oath to bind his soul with a bond," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the vow. Keil and Clarke note the man binding his soul by vow or oath to God (the solemn obligation, the bond on the soul) — the vow (the binding bond).

On "he shall not break his word, he shall do according to all that proceedeth out of his mouth," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the performance. Gill and the grammatical-historical note the duty to perform the vow, not breaking the word (the sanctity of the word, Ecclesiastes 5:4-5; Psalm 15:4) — the figure (the faithfulness that keeps its word); the providence (the law of the man's vow, the vows of women to follow, vv.3-15). The verse displays the binding of a man's vow and the sanctity of his word.

The witnesses agree the substance: a man who vows to God is bound to perform it and must not break his word (Calvin, Keil, Clarke, Gill). The verse displays the vow of a young woman in her father's house (vv.3-5).

5:
"But if her father disallow her in the day that he heareth; not any of her vows, or of her bonds wherewith she hath bound her soul, shall stand: and the LORD shall forgive her, because her father disallowed her." The witnesses note the father's power to annul the daughter's vow and the LORD's forgiving her — the crux of the authority that releases and the grace that forgives. On "the LORD shall forgive her, because her father disallowed her," interpreters expound the release. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the annulled vow and the forgiveness — "if her father disallow her in the day that he heareth; not any of her vows... shall stand: and the LORD shall forgive her, because her father disallowed her": if the father, on the day he heard, forbade the vow, it did not stand, and God forgave her the non-performance; the witnesses note the significance — the authority of the father to annul the vow, and God's gracious release of the daughter from the bond (the father's headship overriding the vow, and the LORD not holding her guilty for what was not in her power to perform); the lesson — the gentleness of God's law, that the one under authority is not bound by a vow her head forbids, and is forgiven the breaking (the forgiveness "because her father disallowed her," the release granted by God himself); the truth that obligation rests on what is in one's power, and God does not require what authority has annulled; the figure — the grace that forgives where the bond is lawfully released, the figure of the headship that may release from a rash vow, and the God who forgives the released, "the LORD shall forgive her"; The figure of the authority that releases and the LORD who forgives.

"But if her husband hath utterly made them void on the day he heard them; then whatsoever proceeded out of her lips concerning her vows, or concerning the bond of her soul, shall not stand: her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her." The very brief verse notes the husband making the wife's vow void and the LORD forgiving her. The grammatical voices note the release. Clarke and Gill note that "if her husband hath utterly made them void on the day he heard them; then whatsoever proceeded out of her lips... shall not stand: her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her" — if the husband annulled the vow on the day he heard, it did not stand, and God forgave her; the if her husband hath utterly made them void on the day he heard them (the husband annulling); the whatsoever proceeded out of her lips... shall not stand (the vow void); the her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her (the release and forgiveness); the figure (the husband's authority making the vow void, and God's gracious release of the wife; the truth that the wife is not bound by a vow her husband annuls, and is forgiven, the same gentleness as for the daughter, vv.5; the figure of the headship that releases and the God who forgives; the order of the household and the grace of forgiveness). The verse holds together the release and forgiveness.

On "her husband hath made them void; and the LORD shall forgive her," the grammatical-historical Clarke and grammatical-historical note the forgiveness: "the LORD shall forgive her" — God forgiving the wife the non-performance (the grace that releases); the figure (the God who forgives the lawfully released); the providence (the release, the rule of the husband's silence or annulling to follow, vv.13-15). The verse displays the husband making the wife's vow void and the LORD forgiving her.

13:
"Every vow, and every binding oath to afflict the soul, her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void." The witnesses note the husband's authority to establish or annul every vow of his wife — the crux of the headship over the wife's vows. On "her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void," interpreters expound the authority. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the headship — "every vow, and every binding oath to afflict the soul, her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void": the husband held the authority to confirm or annul every vow or self-affliction his wife had bound on herself; the witnesses note the significance — the husband's headship over the wife's vows (the authority of the husband, as the head, to establish the vow by his consent or to make it void by his prohibition); the lesson — the order of the household under God, the wife's vows subject to the husband's judgment (especially vows "to afflict the soul," fastings and abstinences, that the husband might temper or release; the head responsible for the household, the wife's obligations not independent of him); the truth that God ordained order in the family, the husband over the wife, as Christ over the church, "the husband is the head of the wife, even as Christ is the head of the church," Ephesians 5:23; the figure — the headship that may confirm or release, a figure of the authority God appoints in the household; the wisdom of the law that the wife's vow be not a snare to the household but subject to the head; the figure of the order of the family, the husband's authority over the wife's vows, tempered by love and responsibility. The figure of the headship that establishes or releases the wife's vow.

On "every vow, and every binding oath to afflict the soul," the grammatical-historical Clarke and witnesses note the vows. Clarke and Gill note the vows and self-afflictions (fastings, abstinences) the wife had bound on herself (the binding oaths) — the vows (to afflict the soul).

On "her husband may establish it, or her husband may make it void," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the authority. Gill and the grammatical-historical note the husband's authority to confirm or annul every vow, the headship over the wife's vows (the order of the household, Ephesians 5:23) — the figure (the authority God appoints in the family, as Christ over the church); the providence (the rule, the consequence of the husband's silence or delay to follow, vv.14-15). The verse displays the husband's authority to establish or annul every vow of his wife.

The verse displays the rule of the husband's silence and delay (vv.14-15).
14:
"But if her husband altogether hold his peace at her from day to day; then he establisheth all her vows, or all her bonds, which are upon her: he confirmeth them, because he held his peace at her in the day that he heard them." The very brief verse notes the husband's continued silence establishing the wife's vows. The grammatical voice notes the confirmation. Gill notes that "if her husband altogether hold his peace at her from day to day; then he establisheth all her vows... because he held his peace at her in the day that he heard them" — if the husband kept silence day after day, he thereby established the vows; the if her husband altogether hold his peace at her from day to day (the continued silence); the then he establisheth all her vows, or all her bonds (the vows confirmed by silence); the because he held his peace at her in the day that he heard them (the silence on the day of hearing the ground); the figure (the husband's silence over time taken as confirmation, the vow established; the truth that the head who hears and is silent confirms, the consent given by silence; the figure of the responsibility of the head, his silence binding the vow; the order that the time of decision is the day of hearing, the silence then confirming). The verse holds together the confirmation by continued silence.

The verse displays the husband's continued silence establishing the wife's vows.

15:
"But if he shall any ways make them void after that he hath heard them; then he shall bear her iniquity." The very brief verse notes the husband bearing the guilt if he annuls the vow after confirming it by silence. The grammatical voices note the responsibility. Wesley and Gill note that "if he shall any ways make them void after that he hath heard them; then he shall bear her iniquity" — if the husband, having confirmed the vow by silence, later annulled it, he bore the guilt of its non-performance; the if he shall any ways make them void after that he hath heard them (the late annulling, after confirmation); the then he shall bear her iniquity (the husband answerable); the figure (the husband responsible for the broken vow if he annuls it after the time of decision; the truth that the head who confirms by silence and then forbids bears the guilt of the breaking; the figure of the responsibility of authority, the head answerable for the household's vows; the warning that the husband not deal deceitfully, confirming then annulling, lest he bear the iniquity; the order that the time of annulling is the day of hearing, and the late annulling lays the guilt on him). The verse holds together the husband's responsibility.

On "if he shall any ways make them void after that he hath heard them," the witnesses note the late annulling. Gill notes the husband annulling after confirming by silence (the late annulling) — the annulling (after the time).

On "then he shall bear her iniquity," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the guilt: "then he shall bear her iniquity" — the husband answerable for the broken vow (the responsibility of the head); the figure (the head bearing the guilt of the breaking); the providence (the responsibility, the summary of the law to follow, vv.16). The verse displays the husband bearing the guilt if he annuls the vow after confirming it.

16:
"These are the statutes, which the LORD commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between the father and his daughter, being yet in her youth in her father's house." The very brief verse notes the summary of the law of vows in the household. The grammatical voices note the summary. Clarke and Gill note that "these are the statutes, which the LORD commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, between the father and his daughter, being yet in her youth in her father's house" — the chapter closes by summing up the statutes of vows in the relations of the household; the these are the statutes, which the LORD commanded Moses (the divine law summed); the between a man and his wife (the husband and wife); the between the father and his daughter, being yet in her youth (the father and the young daughter); the figure (the law of vows ordered within the relations of the household, the husband and wife, the father and daughter; the truth that God's law touches the order of the family, the authority and the obligation; the figure of the wisdom of God's law in the household, the vows tempered by the order of authority; the statutes that secure both the sanctity of vows and the order of the family, the law complete). The verse closes the chapter, before the war on Midian.
